title: React developer tools

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for React developer tools; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to React developer tools
sources.claim: React DevTools inspect component trees, props, state, context, and profiling for a particular development render.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React DevTools inspect component trees, props, state, context, and profiling for a particular development render.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A profiler shows an expensive list rerender before memoization is considered and measured again. Your goal is to locate and explain a component data-flow defect using developer-tool evidence. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: javascript project browser app; Declarative UI and re-rendering; javascript runtime devtools. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React DevTools inspect component trees, props, state, context, and profiling for a particular development render. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Inspection can expose personal data, profiling changes timing, and observed rerenders are not automatically performance defects. This draft therefore rejects: Every highlighted rerender must be removed with memoization.

includes: Inspect component trees, props, state and render causes without editing production state as a fix.
Locate and explain a component data-flow defect using developer-tool evidence.
excludes: Inspection can expose personal data, profiling changes timing, and observed rerenders are not automatically performance defects.

outcomes: Locate and explain a component data-flow defect using developer-tool evidence.

examples.title: React developer tools: concrete case
examples.situation: A profiler shows an expensive list rerender before memoization is considered and measured again.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A profiler shows an expensive list rerender before memoization is considered and measured again.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use DevTools measurements to find costly work; rerendering itself is normal and memoization needs evidence.
examples.result: The result follows because Use DevTools measurements to find costly work; rerendering itself is normal and memoization needs evidence.

examples.title: React developer tools: boundary case
examples.situation: Inspection can expose personal data, profiling changes timing, and observed rerenders are not automatically performance defects.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Inspection can expose personal data, profiling changes timing, and observed rerenders are not automatically performance defects.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Every highlighted rerender must be removed with memoization.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use DevTools measurements to find costly work; rerendering itself is normal and memoization needs evidence.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every highlighted rerender must be removed with memoization.
misconceptions.correction: Use DevTools measurements to find costly work; rerendering itself is normal and memoization needs evidence.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Every highlighted rerender must be removed with memoization.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model React developer tools, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for React developer tools before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to locate and explain a component data-flow defect using developer-tool evidence. Keep this boundary in view: Inspection can expose personal data, profiling changes timing, and observed rerenders are not automatically performance defects.
retrievalPrompts: Explain React developer tools using exact React: Component-Based Browser Applications terms.
Why is this claim incorrect: Every highlighted rerender must be removed with memoization.
Apply checkpoint 'measurements' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Function components”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
